evidence_quotes.quote: certain game emulators necessary for legal game preservation

I think the digital fairness act needs to legally ensure that the user is allowed to install and use any software on the products they legally purchase without needing them to be verified or approved by the OEM.

paragraphs.text: I think the digital fairness act needs to legally ensure that the user is allowed to install and use any software on the products they legally purchase without needing them to be verified or approved by the OEM. This is in response to Google threatening to only ensure apps verified by them can be installed and used on their Android platform, including those obtained by "sideloading" apps from sources that are not Google's Play Store. This is a deliberate effort to control user's freedom to use apps Google does not want them to on the phones they pay for and own, under the guise of protecting user safety. Their explanation of their verification system being like "an ID check at the airport" makes 0 sense, as unlike airports which are very public places where the safety of many people are at risk, a phone is a private device that only affects a single user, or at most a couple of users. Google should not have to check a developers "ID" to enter a user's private "airport" that does not impact anyone else. If a user wants to risk obtaining malware by "sideloading" or downloading apps from developers not "verified" by Google, it should by their right to do so if they fully understand the risks. As Android currently stands, if you allow the installation of applications from "sideloading" or installing apps from websites directly, you have to go into settings and turn off already pre-existing safety settings. Any user doing so most likely knows the risks, knows what they are doing, and is prepared to face the consequences of whatever happens to their phone if dangerous software is installed. Google should not have control over what software users are able to access on their phones. In many totalitarian countries where certain services and information are restricted, Google complies with certain draconian laws and does not allow the installation of certain apps via the Google Play Store in certain regions. "Sideloading", or directly installing apps from websites, represents the only way for users to access certain apps. In addition, this decision allows Google to stifle competition on their own platform, shutting down open source competitors for the software of them and their partners, such as open source calendar apps, alternate Youtube clients, F-Droid as an alternate app store, alternate keyboards they don't personally like, certain game emulators necessary for legal game preservation, and others. If Google wants to add this verification system, I am proposing that it be required by law to provide users a method of turning off this system, allowing users to install apps directly from websites, "sideload apps", or otherwise use applications not directly approved of by Google. It is my right, as the owner of a phone running the Android operating system, to use whichever applications I wish to regardless of any "approval" by certain tech corporations, so long as those applications are legal and do no harm to individuals.
